word_list.word: honor and profit lie not in one sack
word_list.pronunciation: 藞蓱藧n蓺晒 忙nd p晒藞蓱藧f瑟t l藞a瑟 n藢蓱藧t 瑟n w藞蕦n s藞忙k

senses.definition: used to suggest that  it is difficult to achieve both financial success and maintain a high level of moral or ethical standards at the same time

examples.x: My friend learned the hard way that honor and profit lie not in one sack when he agreed to do a favor for a client that compromised his values, only to lose their business in the end.

word_list.word: make yourself all honey and the flies will devour you
word_list.pronunciation: m藢e瑟k jo藧晒s藞蓻lf 藞蓴藧l h藞蕦ni 忙nd 冒蓹 fl藞a瑟z w瑟l d瑟v藞a瑟蕣晒 ju藧

senses.definition: used to suggest that some people might take advantage of those who are very kind, gentle, or always willing to please others

examples.x: As a leader, it's important to be confident and assertive, because make yourself all honey and the flies will devour you.

word_list.word: if your friend is honey, do not lick him thoroughly
word_list.pronunciation: 瑟f j蕣晒 f晒藞蓻nd 瑟z h藞蕦ni du藧n藢蓱藧t l藞瑟k h藢瑟m 胃藞蕦晒o蕣li

senses.definition: used to warn against taking advantage of a friend's kindness or generosity to the point of overindulgence or exploitation

word_list.word: he that will steal an egg will steal an ox
word_list.pronunciation: hi藧 冒忙t w瑟l st藞i藧l 蓯n 藞蓻伞 w瑟l st藞i藧l 蓯n 藞蓱藧ks

senses.definition: used to imply that a person who is willing to commit a small act of dishonesty or theft is likely to commit more significant acts

examples.x: The employer decided not to hire the candidate who had been caught stealing office supplies because they believed that he that will steal an egg will steal an ox.

word_list.word: corporations have neither bodies to be punished, nor souls to be damned
word_list.pronunciation: k藢蓴藧晒p蓺晒藞e瑟蕛蓹nz h忙v n藞i藧冒蓺 b藞蓱藧d瑟z t蓹bi p藞蕦n瑟蕛t n藞蓴藧晒 s藞o蕣lz t蓹bi d藞忙md

senses.definition: used to imply that corporations may be able to engage in unethical or immoral behavior without facing the same consequences as individuals who engage in similar behavior

word_list.word: it (sure|) is easy to find a stick to beat a dog
word_list.pronunciation: 瑟t 蕛藞蕣晒 瑟z 藞i藧zi t蓹 f藞a瑟nd 蓯 st藞瑟k t蓹 b藞i藧t 蓯 d藞蓱藧伞

senses.definition: used to suggest that it is easy to find fault or criticize someone, especially someone vulnerable, even if they have done nothing wrong or the fault is minor

word_list.word: all lay loads on a willing horse
word_list.pronunciation: 藞蓴藧l l藞e瑟 l藞o蕣dz 藢蓱藧n 蓯 w藞瑟l瑟艐 h藞蓴藧晒s

senses.definition: used to suggest that people tend to take advantage of those who are generous and willing to help, often without considering their own responsibilities or limitations

examples.x: John is a hard worker who never says no to anyone, but he needs to learn that all lay loads on a willing horse, and he should set boundaries.

word_list.word: lying a little and stealing a little will get you nicely through the world
word_list.pronunciation: l藞a瑟瑟艐 蓯 l藞瑟删蓹l 忙nd st藞i藧l瑟艐 蓯 l藞瑟删蓹l w瑟l 伞蓻t ju藧 n藞a瑟sli 胃晒u藧 冒蓹 w藞蓽藧ld

senses.definition: used to suggest that dishonesty and theft can be useful strategies for achieving success
